The entire human race, except for Noah and his family, was now dead, so God remembering Noah meant his mercy had returned to humanity, which he would not completely destroy. The demands of divine justice were met by the destruction of sinners. God sent his wind to dry the earth and seal up his waters. The same power that brings devastation also brings deliverance; therefore, we must always look to that power. Afflictions will be removed when they have accomplished their purpose, whether it is destruction or healing. Just as the earth was not drowned in a day, it was not dried in a day. God usually provides for his people’s deliverance gradually, so that small beginnings are not disdained and great achievements are not feared.

The ark rested on a mountain, directed there by God's wise and gracious providence to allow for quicker rest. God has times and places of rest for his people after hardship; and often he provides for their comfortable settlement without their planning or foresight. God had told Noah when the flood would come, but did not reveal by revelation at what times and in what ways it would recede. Knowing about the beginning was necessary to build the ark; knowing about the end would only satisfy curiosity and would exercise his faith and patience. Noah sent a raven from the ark, which flew around and fed on floating carcasses. He then sent a dove, which returned the first time without good news, but the second time brought an olive leaf in its beak, clearly showing that trees, fruit trees, were beginning to appear above water. Noah sent the dove again seven days later, and a third time after another seven days, likely on the Sabbath day. Having kept the Sabbath with his small church, he expected special blessings from Heaven and inquired about them. The dove is an emblem of a gracious soul that, finding no true peace in this deluged and defiling world, returns to Christ as its ark, its Noah, its rest. The carnal heart, like the raven, takes up with the world and feeds on what it finds there; but return you to my rest, O my soul, to your Noah, so it is said. And just as Noah reached out and took the dove into the ark, Christ will save, help, and welcome those who flee to him for rest.

God considers our benefit rather than our desires; he knows what is good for us better than we do ourselves, and how long restraints should continue and desired mercies should be delayed. We would want to leave the ark before the ground is dry; and perhaps, if the door is shut, we are ready to push aside the covering and find another way out; but God’s time for showing mercy is the best time. Just as Noah received a command to enter the ark, so he would wait for a command to exit it again, no matter how tedious his confinement was. We must acknowledge God in all our ways and keep him before us in all our moves. Only those who follow God’s direction and submit to him are under his protection.

Noah now went out into a desolate world, where one might have thought his first concern would be to build a house for himself, but he began with an altar for God. It is good to begin with God. Although Noah's livestock was small and saved at great care and expense, he did not begrudge giving to God from it. Serving God with what little we have increases it; we should never think that anything is wasted when honored by God. The first thing done in the new world was an act of worship. We are now to express our thankfulness, not through burnt offerings, but through praise, pious devotion and conversation. God was well pleased with what was done. But the burning flesh could no more please God than the blood of bulls and goats, except as a symbol of Christ’s sacrifice, expressing Noah's humble faith and dedication to God. The flood washed away the race of wicked men, but it did not remove sin from human nature, who being conceived and born in sin, thinks, devises, and loves wickedness, even from his youth, just as much since the flood as before. But God graciously declared he would never drown the world again. While the earth remains and people live on it, there will be summer and winter. It is clear that this earth is not to remain forever; it and all its works must shortly be burned up; and we look forward to new heavens and a new earth when all these things shall be dissolved. But as long as it does remain, God’s providence will cause the course of times and seasons to continue, and make each know its place. And on this word we depend, that it shall be so. We see God's promises to creation fulfilled, and may infer that his promises to all believers will also be.
